policy_statement: Account for land-use needs of centralised and regional renewable energy production.
mechanism_description: It will take renewable energy land-use needs into account to help enable renewable energy project siting and buildout, supporting displacement of fossil electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use planning/permitting → ↑ renewable project deployment → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: City/regional land-use plans, zoning, and permitting proactively allocate/identify suitable areas and grid/space corridors for renewable energy infrastructure (e.g., solar, wind, associated substations/cabling), reducing development friction and conflict.  
(2) Process: Lower siting constraints and faster approvals increase realized renewable capacity and generation; marginal renewable generation substitutes for fossil-based generation on the grid.  
(3) Outcome: Reduced combustion of coal/oil/gas for electricity leads to lower CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions, yielding mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: Enabling renewables siting and deployment increases zero/low-carbon electricity supply, displacing fossil electricity generation and reducing net GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Land-use planning enables renewable deployment, which primarily reduces power-sector emissions via fossil displacement.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although framed as “planning” (a governance tool) and can have co-benefits (e.g., local air quality, energy security), the single biggest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is GHG mitigation from increased renewable generation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a siting plan were mainly to reduce hazard exposure/resilience of energy assets, it could shift toward adaptation; if primarily to reduce land consumption via repowering/efficiency, it might touch resource efficiency. However, the canonical mechanism explicitly targets enabling renewable buildout to displace fossil generation, so mitigation dominates.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is renewable-energy siting/planning to enable deployment will, by design, act chiefly by increasing renewable generation and reducing fossil generation, making mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific mitigation subtype (e.g., “clean electricity,” “renewable energy permitting reform,” “grid enabling”) depends on policy-level details and is deferred to later evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be partly Nature-Based Solutions if centered on biomass/afforestation-based energy supply, or partly Adaptation if primarily about siting to avoid climate hazards; otherwise straightforward mitigation via renewable deployment enabling.
key_indicators: “land-use needs”; “centralised and regional production of renewable energy”; “will be taken into account”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits include reduced local air pollution and improved energy security, but these are not stated in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: Broad/aspirational phrasing without specifying the planning instrument (e.g., zoning overlays, designated areas, permitting streamlining); classified as a planning-strategy based on land-use integration language rather than an explicit regulatory mandate.
